g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers the five framework activities in software engineering and the principles that guide each activity.
g.text: Principles That Guide Each Framework Activity

g.data-narration: Software engineering uses five framework activities: Communication, Planning, Modeling, Construction, and Deployment. These form the backbone of any software project.
These principles ensure quality, efficiency, and stakeholder satisfaction. They help teams make informed decisions and manage risks throughout the project lifecycle.
g.text: • Ensure quality and reliability in software products
• Enable efficient resource allocation and project management
• Reduce risks and prevent costly errors

g.data-narration: Communication establishes the foundation. Key principles include active listening, questioning, documentation, stakeholder identification, and requirement validation.
Planning creates the project roadmap. Principles include realistic estimation, risk management, work breakdown, milestone definition, and resource allocation.
Modeling visualizes the system before implementation. Principles include abstraction, modularity, completeness, consistency, and traceability.
Construction transforms designs into code. Principles include code quality, early testing, refactoring, peer review, and automation of repetitive tasks.
Deployment delivers software to users. Key principles include user training, support planning, gradual rollout, feedback collection, and having rollback plans.
Example: In a banking system project, the team applies communication principles by actively listening, identifying stakeholders, and validating requirements.
g.text: Scenario: Developing an online banking system
Application: Active Listening to Stakeholder ID to Questioning to Documentation to Req Validation
Outcome: Clear requirements reduce rework and misunderstandings

g.data-narration: Example: For an e-commerce website, planning principles help estimate effort, define milestones, identify risks, and allocate resources effectively.
g.text: Application: Estimation to Work Breakdown to Risk ID to Milestones to Resource Allocation
Outcome: Clear planning helps team stay on track and within budget

g.data-narration: Example: For a hospital management system, modeling principles help create abstract views, ensure modularity, and maintain consistency across designs.
g.text: Scenario: Creating models for a hospital management system
Application: Abstraction to Modularity to Consistency to Traceability to Completeness
Outcome: Clear models guide developers and reduce integration issues

g.data-narration: Framework activities are not strictly sequential. They overlap and iterate throughout the project lifecycle, adapting to changing requirements.
g.text: Framework activities overlap and iterate throughout the project lifecycle. Communication failure cascades into all other activities.
These principles apply universally across different process models: Waterfall, Agile, Spiral, and DevOps.

g.data-narration: Avoid common mistakes: do not skip communication, underestimate planning, skip modeling, treat testing as separate, or ignore post-deployment support.
g.text: • Skipping communication and jumping directly to coding
• Treating testing as a separate phase

g.data-narration: Here is a summary table mapping each framework activity to its key principles for quick revision.
Remember: Communication is the foundation. Planning reduces risk. Model before coding. Test continuously. Deploy includes support. Good luck with your exams.
